bibliographic_data.title: On the Generation, Structure, and Semantics of Grammar Patterns in Source Code Identifiers
bibliographic_data.abstract: Identiﬁers make up a majority of the text in code. They are one of the most basic mediums through which developers describe the code they create and understand the code that others create. Therefore, understanding the patterns latent in identiﬁer naming practices and how accurately we are able to automatically model these patterns is vital if researchers are to support developers and automated analysis approaches in comprehending and creating identiﬁers correctly and optimally. This paper investigates identiﬁers by studying sequences of part-of-speech annotations, referred to as grammar patterns. This work advances our understanding of these patterns and our ability to model them by 1) establishing common naming patterns in diﬀerent types of identiﬁers, such as class and attribute names; 2) analyzing how diﬀerent patterns inﬂuence comprehension; and 3) studying the accuracy of state-of-the-art techniques for part-of-speech annotations, which are vital in automatically modeling identiﬁer naming patterns, in order to establish their limits and paths toward improvement.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper advances our understanding of grammar patterns by establishing common naming patterns in different types of identifiers, analyzing the influence of these patterns on comprehension, and studying the accuracy of state-of-the-art techniques for part-of-speech annotations.
core_content.key_methodology: Manually annotating a dataset of 1,335 identiﬁers from 20 open-source systems to study naming patterns, semantics, and tagger accuracy.
outcomes: Established common grammar patterns in class and attribute names
Analyzed the influence of these patterns on comprehension
Studied the accuracy of state-of-the-art part-of-speech tagging techniques
core_content.full_text_markdown: This paper investigates identiﬁers by studying sequences of part-of-speech annotations, referred to as grammar patterns. The authors manually annotate a dataset of 1,335 identiﬁers from 20 open-source systems and use this dataset to study naming patterns, semantics, and tagger accuracy. They establish common grammar patterns in class and attribute names, analyze the influence of these patterns on comprehension, and study the accuracy of state-of-the-art part-of-speech tagging techniques.
